| **Progress** 09/01/20 to 08/31/22  **Outputs** Target Audience:Researchers using the equipment include: graduate students, staff, postdocs, and faculty in the Institute of Arctic Biology (UAF), the International Arctic Research Center (UAF), Geophysical Institute (UAF), the Water and Environmental Research Center (UAF), and IANRE (UAF), and the Alaska State Division of Forestry. Additional users include citizen scientists that are part of the OneTree Alaska program that is a birch sap cooperative. Changes/Problems:The primary issues have come from COVID delays, including supply chain problems that slowed the shipment of necessary supplies and replacement parts. The order for the equipment arrived months later than anticipated and the company was delayed in training our lab members due to COVID policies (the training must be in-person). Additionally, there were roof repairs on the building that limited our work over the summer of 2022 due to duct work and other problems this created. We have done the best we could and have a plan going forward to ensure the equipment will be used to the fullest capacity. What opportunities for training and professional development has the project provided?Three staff, one undergraduate student, and one faculty recieved training on the IRMS and laser ablation system.Young-Robertson trained 3 students, 2 staff, and 1 faculty on stable isotope basics, including applications, analysis techniques, and sample preparation via a 12 week seminar. Young-Robertson also disseminated information to the OneTree citizen scientists, including sharing sampling supplies and datasheets. They have submitted birch sap samples for isotope analysis that will occur in autumn 2022. How have the results been disseminated to communities of interest?Young-Robertson has disseminated information via meetings with colleagues, participating in the Long Term Ecological Research (LTER) meetings and sharing about equipment capabilities, sharing samping protocols with OneTree for the members to collect sap samples to analyze on the IRMS,and presenting at the Regional Conference on Permafrost in 2022. What do you plan to do during the next reporting period to accomplish the goals?This is the final report, but our work will be ongoing to test the capabilities of the IRMS and gain additional users of the equipment.   **Impacts** What was accomplished under these goals? We achieved the following: - purchased the IRMS,laser ablation unit, elemental analyzer, and gasbench - removed the existing IRMS and Picarro - recieved training on the IRMS and laser ablation system - purchased the necessary commodities and gasses to operate the equipment (with other funding, as this funding does not allow for these purchases) - purchased international standards for all the isotope species this equipment can analyze - developed internal standards that are used with sample runs - developedspreadsheets to quickly and easily import and standardize the data produced by the equipment - tested all the components of the IRMS, including thelaser ablation unit, elemental analyzer, and gasbench - analyzed leaf samples from common boreal species for carbon and nitrogen isotope ratios - tested the laser ablation system on tree rings, determining that micron-sized samples can be analyzed for carbon isotope ratios (capturing weekly growth dynamics of boreal trees) - tested different ways to run small and contaminated water samples, including using the elemental analyzer - trained 3 students, 2 staff, and 1 faculty on stable isotope basics, including applications, analysis techniques, and sample preparation via a 12 week seminar - developed new research in the area of examining the isotope characteristics of common boreal fungi and shrub rings   **Publications** |
| **Progress** 09/01/21 to 08/31/22  **Outputs** Target Audience:Researchers using the equipment include: graduate students, staff, postdocs, and faculty in the Institute of Arctic Biology (UAF), the International Arctic Research Center (UAF), Geophysical Institute (UAF), the Water and Environmental Research Center (UAF), and IANRE (UAF), and the Alaska State Division of Forestry. Additional users include citizen scientists that are part of the OneTree Alaska program that is a birch sap cooperative. Changes/Problems:The primary issues have come from COVID delays, including supply chain problems that slowed the shipment of necessary supplies and replacement parts. Additionally, there were roof repairs on the building that limited our work over the summer of 2022 due to duct work and other problems this created. We have done the best we could and have a plan going forward to ensure the equipment will be used to the fullest capacity. What opportunities for training and professional development has the project provided?Young-Robertson trained 3 students, 2 staff, and 1 faculty on stable isotope basics, including applications, analysis techniques, and sample preparation via a 12 week seminar. Young-Robertson also disseminated information to the OneTree citizen scientists, including sharing sampling supplies and datasheets. They have submitted birch sap samples for isotope analysis that will occur in autumn 2022. How have the results been disseminated to communities of interest?Young-Robertson has disseminated information via meetings with colleagues, participating in the Long Term Ecological Research (LTER) meetings and sharing about equipment capabilities, sharing samping protocols with OneTree for the members to collect sap samples to analyze on the IRMS, and presenting at the Regional Conference on Permafrost in 2022. What do you plan to do during the next reporting period to accomplish the goals?There are no additional reporting periods; this is the last one.   **Impacts** What was accomplished under these goals? We achieved the following in this reporting period: -purchased the necessary commodities and gasses to operate the equipment (with other funding, as this funding does not allow for these purchases) - purchased international standards for all the isotope species this equipment can analyze - developed internal standards that are used with sample runs - developed spreadsheets to quickly and easily import and standardize the data produced by the equipment - tested all the components of the IRMS, including the laser ablation unit, elemental analyzer, and gas bench - analyzed leaf samples from common boreal species for carbon and nitrogen isotope ratios - tested the laser ablation system on tree rings, determining that micron-sized samples can be analyzed for carbon isotope ratios (capturing weekly growth dynamics of boreal trees) - tested different ways to run small and contaminated water samples, including using the elemental analyzer - trained 3 students, 2 staff, and 1 faculty on stable isotope basics, including applications, analysis techniques, and sample preparation via a 12 week seminar - developed new research in the area of examining the isotope characteristics of common boreal fungi and shrub rings   **Publications** |
| **Progress** 09/01/20 to 08/31/21  **Outputs** Target Audience:Due to COVID-19, there have been numerous delays, including (1) getting the equipment purchased, (2) the company building the equipment (supply chain delays), (3) the company representatives coming to UAF to install the equipment, (4) the company sending someone to train our personnel, and (5) supply chain issues for various parts and pieces that are needed to complete the assembly. That being said, Young-Robertson has made 3 presentations about the equipment and its capabilities in order to bring in users when the equipment is fully operational. The audiences that have been reached are: academic researchers without and outside UAF (including the Woodwell Climate Research Center, the Alaska Climate Adaptation Science Center, the Bonanza Creek LTER, IANRE advisory group, and other UAF research personnell) and our collaborators at the Alaska State Division of Forestry and the Moose Research Station. Changes/Problems:Due to COVID-19, there have been numerous delays, including (1) getting the equipment purchased, (2) the company building the equipment (supply chain delays), (3) the company representatives coming to UAF to install the equipment, (4) the company sending someone to train our personnel, and (5) supply chain issues for various parts and pieces that are needed to complete the assembly. Due to these issues, we are 6 to 8 months behind of where we hoped to be at this time. However, we are making strides to catch upand test the equipment capabilities, share information with the science community, and bring in users as fast as we can. What opportunities for training and professional development has the project provided?Two technicians were trained to operate the equipment, process data, troubleshoot problems, and learn the full capacity of the mass spec and its interfacing pieces. We also started a lab discussion group, including students, to read an ecologically based stable isotope book in order to learn about the various applications of this tool for research. How have the results been disseminated to communities of interest?Since we have just started to run samples to test the capability of the equipment, we do not have results to share yet. What do you plan to do during the next reporting period to accomplish the goals?We will be running organic samples on the elemental analyzer in the coming weeks, and we will start to test the other components such as the laser ablation system as soon as the remaining parts arrive.   **Impacts** What was accomplished under these goals? Due to COVID-19, there have been numerous delays, including (1) getting the equipment purchased, (2) the company building the equipment (supply chain delays), (3) the company representatives coming to UAF to install the equipment, (4) the company sending someone to train our personnel, and (5) supply chain issues for various parts and pieces that are needed to complete the assembly. That being said, the equipment has been installed and personnell have been trained. We are waiting on three parts that are delayed due to supply chain issues. We have purchased all the international and internal standards, gasses, and other consumables.The elemental analyzer is ready to be used and is being tested on plant and soil samples in the coming 2 weeks. These samples will be analyzed for isotopes of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and hydrogen. We will analyze them for sulfur in January. Once we get the remaining parts, we will start testing the laser ablation system on tree cores and the gas bench/autosampler frontend on water samples.   **Publications** |

Themis: Research Journal of Justice Studies and Forensic Themis: Research Journal of Justice Studies and Forensic Science Science Volume 12 Issue 1 Article 4 5-19-2024 The Limitations and Future of Isotope Analysis in Forensic The Limitations and Future of Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology Anthropology Kris Weller San Jose State University Follow this and additional works at: https://scholarworks.sjsu.edu/themis Part of the Forensic Science and Technology Commons, and the Other Anthropology Commons Recommended Citation Recommended Citation Weller, Kris (2024) "The Limitations and Future of Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology," Themis: Research Journal of Justice Studies and Forensic Science: Vol. 12 : Iss. 1 , Article 4. DOI: https://doi.org/10.55917/2324-6561.1123 https://scholarworks.sjsu.edu/themis/vol12/iss1/4 This Peer-Reviewed Article is brought to you for free and open access by the Justice Studies at SJSU ScholarWorks. It has been accepted for inclusion in Themis: Research Journal of Justice Studies and Forensic Science by an authorized editor of SJSU ScholarWorks. For more information, please contact scholarworks@sjsu.edu. The Limitations and Future of Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology The Limitations and Future of Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology Abstract Abstract This research paper will discuss the applications of isotope analysis in forensic anthropology, focusing on the limitations of this technique and its potential future uses. Isotope analysis is based on the principle that isotope ratios vary across geographic regions and that humans incorporate those unique isotope ratios into their bodies when they eat and drink. Forensic anthropology uses isotope analysis to determine a decedent’s approximate region of origin or residence. It is especially in cases where the remains are partially or highly decomposed, and when other avenues of investigation have been exhausted. In its current form, the applications of isotope analysis to forensic anthropology are limited in their precision and can be affected by numerous confounding factors. Understanding these limitations is a crucial step toward this technique being generally accepted as evidence in criminal courts. Due to the novelty of its application, there is also a lack of reference data, standard operating procedures, and funding for future research. For isotope analysis in forensic anthropology to reach its potential, these limitations and oversights must be addressed by the forensic anthropology community. Keywords Keywords isotope analysis, forensic anthropology, geographic variation, decedent origin, limitations This peer-reviewed article is available in Themis: Research Journal of Justice Studies and Forensic Science: https://scholarworks.sjsu.edu/themis/vol12/iss1/4 The Limitations and Future of Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology 1 The Limitations and Future of Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology Kris Weller Department of Justice Studies, San Jose State University March 22, 2024 1 Weller: Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology Published by SJSU ScholarWorks, 2024 Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology 2 Abstract This research paper will discuss the applications of isotope analysis in forensic anthropology, focusing on the limitations of this technique and its potential future uses. Isotope analysis is based on the principle that isotope ratios vary across geographic regions and that humans incorporate those unique isotope ratios into their bodies when they eat and drink. Forensic anthropology uses isotope analysis to determine a decedent’s approximate region of origin or residence. It is especially in cases where the remains are partially or highly decomposed, and when other avenues of investigation have been exhausted. In its current form, the applications of isotope analysis to forensic anthropology are limited in their precision and can be affected by numerous confounding factors. Understanding these limitations is a crucial step toward this technique being generally accepted as evidence in criminal courts. Due to the novelty of its application, there is also a lack of reference data, standard operating procedures, and funding for future research. For isotope analysis in forensic anthropology to reach its potential, these limitations and oversights must be addressed by the forensic anthropology community. Keywords: isotope analysis, forensic anthropology, geographic variation, decedent origin, limitations 2 Themis: Research Journal of Justice Studies and Forensic Science, Vol. 12 [2024], Iss. 1, Art. 4 https://scholarworks.sjsu.edu/themis/vol12/iss1/4 DOI: 10.55917/2324-6561.1123 Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology 3 The Limitations and Future of Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology For decades, archaeologists have used isotope analysis to track the movements and diets of ancient humans through their skeletal remains (Makarewicz & Sealy, 2015). Isotope analysis of human remains uses the natural variation of isotope ratios found in human tissues to identify where a person was located at different points in their life (Chesson et al., 2018). Recently, this technique has been used to gather geo-temporal information about the premortem movements of unidentified, highly decomposed, or partial remains (Bartelink et al., 2016). The scientific basis of isotope analysis relies on the natural variance of multiple stable isotopes over geographical areas. Isotopes are atoms with the same number of protons but a different number of neutrons (i.e. atoms that are the same element but have different atomic weights). Some isotopes are radioactive and decay into lighter isotopes over time, while others are stable and do not decay (Chesson et al., 2018). Due to their unchanging nature, isotope analysis of human remains is performed on stable isotopes (Baretlink & Chesson, 2019). These isotopes exist in natural ratios; for example, about 99% of carbon atoms exist as 12C or carbon-12 and about 1% exist as 13C (Baretlink & Chesson, 2019). The exact ratio of 12C/13C in humans varies from place to place due to climate, local diet, etc. (Chesson et al., 2018). After consuming isotopes in food and water, the human body uses these elements to create new tissues, such as hair, bone, or teeth (Chesson et al., 2018). There are two types of isotopes analyzed for provenance: organic isotopes and trace metal isotopes. Organic isotopes include carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur. Oxygen and hydrogen isotopes are obtained primarily from drinking water and provide geographical information (Chesson et al., 2018). Carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur are obtained primarily from food, and thus provide information about diet (Chesson et al., 2018). The trace metal isotope 3 Weller: Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology Published by SJSU ScholarWorks, 2024 Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology 4 most commonly used in forensic anthropology is strontium, which originates from bedrock and is “strongly geographically defined” (Makarewicz & Sealy, 2015, para. 22). Lead is also being explored as a useful isotope, though is not as widely used or studied (Bartelink & Chesson, 2019). These differing ratios of isotopes consumed in food and water are what allow scientists to match a person’s tissues with the geographical areas those isotopes may have originated. The information gained about a person’s residence and travel history before death can significantly impact the investigation into their identity and the circumstances surrounding their death (Bartelink et al., 2016). Like with any technique used in forensic science, there is a great deal of scrutiny regarding the reliability of isotope analysis and the scope of information it can provide(Chesson et al., 2021). The application of isotope analysis in forensic anthropology has the potential to provide crucial data when other avenues of identification are exhausted, but requires further research and standardization to ameliorate significant issues with accuracy, precision, and applicability. Literature Review Applications The primary application of isotope analysis in forensic anthropology is using isotope ratios to gain information as to a decedent’s whereabouts before death (Bartelink & Chesson, 2019). This is achieved by analyzing and compiling isotope ratios found in various human tissues into isotope landscapes, or isoscapes (Chesson et al., 2018). These isoscapes are visual maps of the areas identified as possible places of residence or travel by the isotope analysis. Living organisms incorporate the isotopes found in the food and water they consume into their tissues when those tissues are created. Because tissues are formed at many stages of life and are replaced at varying rates, different tissues can provide information about different periods of 4 Themis: Research Journal of Justice Studies and Forensic Science, Vol. 12 [2024], Iss. 1, Art. 4 https://scholarworks.sjsu.edu/themis/vol12/iss1/4 DOI: 10.55917/2324-6561.1123 Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology 5 time before death. The three primary tissues sampled for isotope analysis are keratin from hair and nails, collagen from bones, and bioapatite from bones and teeth. Hair and nails are both fast-growing tissues and thus contain isotopes from the past weeks and months before death. Hair grows at a rate of about 0.4 mm per day, fingernails grow about 2-3 mm per month, and toenails grow about 1 mm per month (Bartelink et al., 2019). Each of these tissues records the isotope ratios as it is created, so it can be used to create a sequence of those ratios by analyzing incremental sections of the hair/nail. For example, “individuals who are sedentary may show similar isotope ratios throughout serial sections of hair or nails, whereas a traveler may show values that change regularly” (Bartelink et al., 2019, para. 34). Depending on the length of hair/nail, isotope analysis can give week by week information from death, up to about 15 months before death (Bartelink & Chesson, 2019). Because hair has better reference data sets available, it is preferred over nails for incremental analysis when possible (Chesson et al., 2018). Bone collagen is a protein that incorporates isotopes primarily from dietary protein (Bartelink & Chesson, 2019). This means the isotopes tested from bone collagen are usually carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur, and do not represent the whole diet or isotopes from drinking water. Bones and teeth contain the biomineral hydroxyapatite, sometimes called bioapatite, which contains isotopes from the whole diet and drinking water (Bartelink & Chesson, 2019). Bioapatite contains oxygen and strontium isotopes, the two types of isotopes most useful for provenancing human remains (Bartelink & Chesson, 2019; Chesson et al., 2018). Because bones continue to replace themselves throughout a person’s lifetime, the isotopes within bone tissue provide location information from 5-25 years before death (Bartelink & Chesson, 2019). Teeth are particularly useful because they are formed during early childhood, or if third molars are 5 Weller: Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology Published by SJSU ScholarWorks, 2024 Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology 6 present, adolescence. By performing isotope analysis on teeth, scientists can determine approximately where a person was born and spent their infancy (Bartelink & Chesson, 2019). When performing isotope analysis, the tissues tested and isotopes analyzed have a significant effect on what conclusions can be drawn from the resulting data. Limitations At the moment, the greatest limitations in the use of isotope analysis in forensic anthropology are the lack of precision, lack of reference materials, lack of standardization, and the presence of confounding factors. It is necessary for forensic scientists to fully comprehend these limitations to draw conclusions that are supported by the available evidence. Only then can new ways to address these limitations be developed. Precision The primary limitation of isotope analysis and isoscapes is their relative lack of precision. Generally, this type of isotope analysis cannot narrow down the area of possible residence to a single neighborhood or city (Bartelink et al., 2014). Depending on the quality of the sample, the presence of confounding factors, sample preparation, instrumentation, and statistical models used, the identified area of residence may include multiple states, prefectures, regions, or countries (Kamenov & Curtis, 2017; Chesson et al., 2021). Though there is enough variation in isotope ratios to use them to investigate place-of-origin, there is still quite a lot of overlap among regions with similar climates (Chesson et al., 2018). This is because the differences in isotope ratios are so small; they are typically reported in parts per thousand rather than percent (Chesson et al., 2018; Bartelink & Chesson, 2019; Dunn et al., 2018). The following figure is a predicted map of oxygen, hydrogen, and deuterium isotope ratios in the contiguous United States: 6 Themis: Research Journal of Justice Studies and Forensic Science, Vol. 12 [2024], Iss. 1, Art. 4 https://scholarworks.sjsu.edu/themis/vol12/iss1/4 DOI: 10.55917/2324-6561.1123 Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology 7 Figure 1 Oxygen, hydrogen, and deuterium isotope ratio maps of the contiguous United States Note. Obtained from Bowen et al., 2007. 7 Weller: Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology Published by SJSU ScholarWorks, 2024 Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology 8 Contrasting Figure 1 with an example of an oxygen isoscape (Figure 2), the accuracy limitations of this type of analysis become clear. Figure 2 Oxygen isoscapes from an unidentified female skull found in the southern California desert Note. Obtained from Bartelink & Chesson, 2019. In Figure 2, an unidentified decedent’s hair was tested using incremental analysis, producing two isoscapes for 13 months before death (Bartelink & Chesson, 2019). Figure A represents possible places of residence 13-3 months before death, while Figure B represents 3 months before death until the time of death (Bartelink & Chesson, 2019). The possible regions of residence in this case were quite large, spanning multiple geographically distant states with similar climates. Nonetheless, new data about the decedent’s patterns of residence (i.e. that she had moved to an area with a different climate about 3 months before death) was gained from this analysis. With isotope analysis, it is crucial to understand the limits of the particular type of analysis performed to draw correct, evidence-based conclusions. For example, the case above only gives possible residence data up to 13 months before death, so no conclusions can be drawn as to her place of birth. 8 Themis: Research Journal of Justice Studies and Forensic Science, Vol. 12 [2024], Iss. 1, Art. 4 https://scholarworks.sjsu.edu/themis/vol12/iss1/4 DOI: 10.55917/2324-6561.1123 Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology 9 Using more than one type of isotope greatly increases the precision of the isoscape. The areas where different isotopes overlap are the areas where residence is most likely, as seen in Figure 3. Figure 3 An example of a multi-element isoscape Note. Obtained from Bartelink & Chesson, 2019. 9 Weller: Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology Published by SJSU ScholarWorks, 2024 Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology 10 These results are not a single smoking gun that will tell investigators the exact city in which a person spent their early childhood. However, the possible areas of origin in this isoscape are much reduced from the previous example. Isotope analysis is not at a point where it can be used to definitively link a person to a small geographical area of origin. That level of precision may never be possible, even if technology and analytic methods improve. Instead, isotope analysis is used in tandem with other investigative tools, such as a biological profile, to narrow down the areas and populations to investigate while searching for a decedent’s identity (Bartelink & Chesson, 2019). Isotope analysis has also been used to rule out regions of origin in certain cases, such as determining whether or not unidentified human remains found near the U.S.-Mexico border were born in the U.S. or Latin America (Bartelink & Chesson, 2019). Reference Data To create precise and accurate isoscapes, it is necessary to amass a large volume of reference data of isotope ratios across geographical areas. Typically, this reference data is gathered either from the environment (e.g. water, foodstuffs, bedrock) or directly from human sources (e.g. hair, bones, teeth) (Bartelink & Chesson, 2018). For the best results, both types of data are gathered and compiled into a model, which is used to create case-specific isoscapes (Chesson et al., 2018). While places like the continental United States and Europe are relatively well mapped, there are large portions of the world—such as the continent of Africa—that currently have little to no recorded isotope ratio data (Hülsemann et al., 2015). This means that isotope analysis is largely unavailable in those regions. Furthermore, individuals who originate or travel from those regions may be statistically less likely to be identified in a missing person’s context. To address this inequality, more isotope data must be gathered from those regions and made available to the scientific community. 10 Themis: Research Journal of Justice Studies and Forensic Science, Vol. 12 [2024], Iss. 1, Art. 4 https://scholarworks.sjsu.edu/themis/vol12/iss1/4 DOI: 10.55917/2324-6561.1123 Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology 11 Lack of Standardization Another limitation of forensic isotope analysis is the lack of standardization. This includes the preparation of samples, analysis methods, and reporting conventions. A study by Pestle et al. found a statistically significant variation in results reported from several labs analyzing the same archaeological sample (2014). This variation came from the differences in preparation and analysis techniques used both within and between the labs. These findings highlight the necessity of standard procedures and statistical models to accurately determine uncertainty, particularly as isotope analysis in forensic anthropology is being introduced to U.S. criminal courts (Chesson et al., 2021). Building from this initial work, Chesson et al. performed a study to develop a consistent and cost-effective method for determining the limits of reasonable variation between laboratories (2021). This method is particularly useful to the field because it can be used to determine “whether or not interpretations made between a sample of interest and a reference database … are sound” (Chesson et al., 2021, para. 39) as well as establish confidence in isotope analysis in both research and the U.S. court system. Additionally, significant progress has been made toward standardizing isotope analysis across disciplines. The Good Practice Guide for Isotope Ratio Mass Spectrometry, which is already in its second edition, includes the best procedures, instrumentation, and reporting conventions when performing isotope analysis in any context (Dunn et al., 2018). There is, however, no real oversight as to the use of these best practices, and each laboratory that performs isotope analysis decides its standard operating procedures (SOPs) and conventions independently (Bartelink & Chesson, 2019; Chesson et al, 2021). Some kind of universally accepted SOPs must be developed and adopted before isotope analysis can be regularly presented as evidence in court. 11 Weller: Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology Published by SJSU ScholarWorks, 2024 Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology 12 Confounding Factors Several confounding factors can affect the results of isotope analysis. These factors influence results by introducing isotopes non-native to the area of origin into a person’s tissues. One such example is the absorption of lead from airborne leaded gasoline pollution (Keller et al., 2016). Given that leaded gasoline was phased out in the U.S. by 1996, this particular issue is likely only present when analyzing the teeth of populations who were young children when leaded gasoline was in widespread use (U.S. Energy Information Administration, 2022). Another example is a diet that is composed of foods and beverages grown or bottled far from the consumer, also called the “supermarket diet” (Bartelink & Chesson, 2019). This modern diet primarily affects the isotopes of carbon and nitrogen; despite this, there still “exists geographic patterning in δ13C values of human hair across the globe” (Bartelink & Chesson, 2019, para. 15; Hülsemann et al., 2015). It is nonetheless important to recognize that modernized diets may affect isotopic composition in some cases. Over time, the environment can contaminate the isotopes in a body’s tissues in a process called diagenesis. This phenomenon may occur when a body is exposed to materials such as soil or groundwater—like when buried—for many years or decades (Bartelink & Chesson, 2019). Hard tissues like bone are more resistant to diagenesis, but hair and nail keratin often degrades significantly after a few months (Bartelink & Chesson, 2019). Analysts prevent diagenesis from affecting their results by screening tissue samples for quality before testing. This is achieved by a number of methods, including quantifying the amount of carbonates in the tissue sample using spectrometry (Bartelink et al., 2014). 12 Themis: Research Journal of Justice Studies and Forensic Science, Vol. 12 [2024], Iss. 1, Art. 4 https://scholarworks.sjsu.edu/themis/vol12/iss1/4 DOI: 10.55917/2324-6561.1123 Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology 13 Conclusion Looking toward the future, isotope analysis has great potential to aid in a variety of investigations, so long as more research is conducted to support this growth. Many of the previously discussed limitations stem from the fact that isotope analysis is relatively new to forensic anthropology, i.e. there has not been time for sufficient research to be conducted. Presently, isotope analysis has been used in only a handful of criminal court cases in the United States, and in those cases was often deemed inadmissible by the court due to its novelty (Chesson et al., 2021). Given the amount of time isotope analysis has been used in other contexts, the scientific foundations of this technique have proven sound. Much of the uncertainty and limitations in its application to forensic anthropology have to do with the lack of reference data, standardization, and affordability. The application of isotope analysis to forensic anthropology will likely remain a somewhat niche—or last-resort—technique until the appropriate SOPs, reference data, and instrumentation are developed. If these issues were addressed, isotope analysis would be more accurate and precise; it would have greater applicability to investigations and admissibility under the Frye and Daubert standards in the U.S. Much of the called-for research is already happening. In 2016, Mambelli et al. published a paper on a more efficient way of analyzing carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur isotopes simultaneously using an isotope ratio mass spectrometer. Novel methods such as these will reduce the workload and cost of isotope analysis. This method in particular also reduces the amount of sample needed, an important consideration when the sample in question is human remains. In 2018, Bataille et al. used machine learning to aid in the creation of a reference isoscape from strontium isotope ratio data in Western Europe. While the use of machine learning 13 Weller: Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology Published by SJSU ScholarWorks, 2024 Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology 14 brings up some ethical and statistical concerns, this method has the potential to reduce the time necessary to process raw data and build isoscapes. Ultimately, any call for more research is also a call for more funding. In particular, funding for the accumulation of reference data sets is often overlooked despite how crucial it is to the reliability of any discipline in forensic science (Baretlink & Chesson, 2019). With the necessary funding to improve data sets, standardization, and precision, isotope analysis has the potential to help identify the thousands of decedents that remain nameless. 14 Themis: Research Journal of Justice Studies and Forensic Science, Vol. 12 [2024], Iss. 1, Art. 4 https://scholarworks.sjsu.edu/themis/vol12/iss1/4 DOI: 10.55917/2324-6561.1123 Isotope Analysis in Forensic Anthropology 15 References Bartelink, E. enriched_points.expanded_content: Isotope analysis requires high-capacity instruments like IRMS, which are capital-intensive, leading to significant financial barriers for labs and researchers in acquiring necessary equipment.
The operation of isotope analysis instruments demands expertise in handling complex machinery and interpreting the results, necessitating trained personnel and ongoing professional development.
Data from isotope analysis can be intricate, requiring sophisticated statistical methods for accurate interpretation, thus necessitating advanced computational skills and domain knowledge.
Variability in sample preparation and analysis techniques across different laboratories creates inconsistencies, highlighting the urgent need for standardized protocols in isotope analysis.
Current isotope analysis methods face challenges in precision and geographical resolution, necessitating further research to improve accuracy and applicability in various scientific fields.
